title: TPU v1 · Timing and Frequency Budget Behind the Systolic Array’s 700 MHz Clock
oneLine: TPU’s timing budget is unusually forgiving: each stage’s critical path is only one 8-bit multiply-accumulate, and 65,536 PEs connect through regular local wiring rather than long global nets. Shallow logic and short wires make 700 MHz comparatively easy to close.

legend.name: In-PE critical path (multiply → add → register)
legend.desc: Each systolic-array PE performs only an 8-bit multiply, accumulation, and register capture within one cycle. The shallow combinational depth gives low stage delay and a generous timing budget.

legend.name: 700 MHz clock budget
legend.desc: The period is approximately 1.43 ns. One shallow MAC plus register setup fits within that cycle, making timing closure straightforward and enabling 700 MHz and ~92 TOPS.

legend.name: Regular local interconnect
legend.desc: In the 256×256 array, every PE connects only to its up, down, left, and right neighbors. These regular, short, lightly loaded wires advance data one stage per cycle without chip-spanning nets.

legend.name: Hypothetical long global wire (slow)
legend.desc: As a contrast, a signal crossing the die has a long wire, heavy load, and large delay; it can become the critical path and reduce frequency. General-purpose CPUs often contain such networks, while the TPU systolic array deliberately avoids them.

steps.cap: ① Decompose one PE’s path: input register → 8b×8b multiply → accumulate → output register. Register-to-register combinational logic contains only one MAC and is very shallow.
② Budget the cycle: logic propagation plus register setup must fit within one clock period. Because the path is shallow, it fits comfortably inside 1.43 ns, supporting 700 MHz.
③ Inspect wiring: the array is regular and each PE connects only to neighbors, producing short, lightly loaded interconnect with low routing delay and no surprise long nets.
④ Contrast the failure mode: a chip-spanning global wire has large capacitance and delay, can become critical, and forces a lower clock. Local systolic wiring naturally avoids that problem.

⑤ Combine the effects: shallow logic (short critical path) × regular local wiring (no long nets) yields predictable timing and easy closure at 700 MHz, unlike a general CPU that repeatedly pipelines and buffers long paths.

before: Set the target frequency and cycle budget: TPU v1 targets 700 MHz, implying a period of ≈1.43 ns. Every register → combinational logic → register path must meet that budget.

Enumerate critical-path candidates: In the systolic array, the primary candidate is multiply → add → register inside a PE. Also check operand-supply and writeback paths at the array boundary for the slowest path on the chip.

Required inputs: The weight-stationary dataflow, with resident weights and cycle-by-cycle activation/partial-sum movement; the 8-bit MAC function; and 28 nm cell-delay and interconnect-RC characteristics. Together they determine stage depth and achievable frequency.

System constraint to resolve: Higher frequency is not automatically better. Along with TDP (28–40 W), area, and DDR3 bandwidth (~30 GB/s), it determines end performance. If memory bandwidth is the roofline bottleneck, raising the clock merely increases idle activity.
during: Core idea: make the critical path shallow enough. The systolic array naturally decomposes matrix multiplication into one MAC per cycle, so each stage contains only a multiply-accumulate and meets 700 MHz without extra timing-driven pipeline stages.

Use regularity for predictable timing: The 256×256 PEs are identical and connect only to neighbors. Placement and routing are regular, wires are short, and loads are uniform, so post-layout timing is predictable and converges quickly.

Avoid long global nets: At 700 MHz, routing often matters more than logic. The array moves data between adjacent PEs one cycle at a time, eliminating die-spanning wires. General CPU bypass networks and global buses have long paths that require aggressive partitioning or buffering.

Trade frequency against pipeline depth: The logic is already shallow, so inserting more registers would add latency, area, and power for little benefit. TPU chooses shallow logic at a moderate frequency and spends its power budget on scale—65,536 MACs—rather than extreme clock speed.

Trade frequency against power and bandwidth: Dynamic power rises with frequency while compute remains constrained by ~30 GB/s of DDR3 bandwidth. TPU pairs 700 MHz with a large 24 MiB Unified Buffer for on-chip reuse instead of relying on frequency alone.

Evidence boundary: The TPU v1 paper does not publish per-stage timing reports, setup/hold margins, clock-tree details, or PVT corners. The conclusion that shallow paths and short wires ease 700 MHz closure follows standard systolic-array timing principles and is consistent with the disclosed 700 MHz/~92 TOPS figures, not disclosed STA data.

result.label: Interconnect
result.value: Regular local short wires; no long global nets

result.label: Contrast with general CPU
result.value: Long critical paths require aggressive pipelining and buffering

after: Run static timing analysis (STA): Across target process, voltage, and temperature corners, verify every register-to-register path meets the 1.43 ns budget with positive setup and hold slack, and confirm the critical path is inside a PE rather than an unexpected long net.
Check array-boundary paths: Separately analyze operand supply, partial-sum writeback, and accumulator/Unified Buffer interfaces. The regular interior may be faster than these less regular edge paths.
Check clock-tree skew: All 65,536 PEs must advance synchronously, so clock-distribution skew must be controlled. This is standard ASIC practice; v1 does not disclose its clock-tree implementation.

Validate the system-level benefit: Insert 700 MHz into the roofline model and ensure the resulting compute is not wasted behind ~30 GB/s DDR3 bandwidth. Frequency must be decided jointly with bandwidth and power.

Deliver and freeze constraints: Hand the validated frequency target, PE critical-path definition, and SDC clock constraints to RTL (⑤) and physical design as the synthesis and place-and-route timing baseline.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (700 MHz/~92 TOPS, the systolic array, and local interconnect are disclosed for v1; per-stage STA margin, clock tree, and PVT corners follow standard datacenter-inference ASIC practice rather than verbatim v1 disclosure)
